description: Why Zenith exists, how its architecture and tooling fit together, who is shaping it, and how to support the work.

intro.title: Built for compile-time clarity.
intro.description: Zenith is a compiler-first UI framework focused on predictable output, structural composition, and minimal runtime cost.
intro.noteText: Zenith is not trying to be another runtime-first framework. It is built to move more complexity into the compiler so browser output stays closer to authored intent.

intro.title: We built it because too much UI work drifts into runtime fog.
intro.description: Zenith exists to keep ownership, output, and browser behavior legible. The goal is not novelty. It is to remove accidental framework machinery from the critical path.

items.title: Accidental runtime complexity
items.summary: Too much work gets deferred into the browser long after the authoring decision was made.
items.detail: Zenith resolves more structure and binding intent at compile time so runtime work stays narrow, explicit, and easier to reason about.

items.title: Too much framework magic
items.summary: Indirection can make composition feel productive while making emitted output harder to trust.
items.detail: Zenith keeps the compiler honest about what each component contributes. It does not rely on hidden component-owned runtime islands to make composition work.

items.title: Ownership should survive composition
items.summary: Reactive ownership and slot scope should not become ambiguous when structure gets deeper.
items.detail: Zenith treats components as structural composition boundaries. Slot content retains parent ownership, and compiler/runtime contracts are enforced around that rule.

items.title: Browsers should receive clear output
items.summary: What authors write should stay legible in the final HTML, bindings, and runtime surface.
items.detail: That means deterministic marker emission, explicit hydration payloads, and a runtime that operates on shipped contracts instead of discovery passes.

intro.title: A small set of principles drives the whole system.
intro.description: Zenith does not treat compiler, runtime, router, bundler, and editor tooling as separate philosophies. The same contract discipline should carry through all of them.

manifesto.label: Framework posture
manifesto.title: Every layer should agree on the same contract.
manifesto.text: Zenith works best when authored structure, compiler guarantees, runtime behavior, and tooling feedback all reinforce one another instead of quietly disagreeing behind the scenes.
manifesto.support: That alignment is what keeps the framework from feeling like one philosophy in the docs and another in the shipped output.

pillars.title: Compile-Time First
pillars.description: Resolve structure, contracts, and more binding work before the browser ever boots.
pillars.support: The compiler is the first place Zenith tries to remove accidental complexity.

pillars.title: Structural Components
pillars.description: Components are composition surfaces, not miniature runtime ownership containers.
pillars.support: They shape output and preserve parent scope instead of silently taking control of it.

pillars.title: Preserved Reactivity
pillars.description: Reactive ownership should remain explicit across slots, refs, and component boundaries.
pillars.support: What updates the DOM should stay attributable to the authoring scope that owns it.

pillars.title: Predictable Output
pillars.description: The emitted HTML, bindings, and runtime markers should stay deterministic and inspectable.
pillars.support: Zenith treats browser output as a contract surface, not an opaque implementation detail.

pillars.title: Explicit Runtime Surface
pillars.description: Runtime work stays narrow and intentional through primitives like hydrate(payload), zenMount(), and zenOn().
pillars.support: The goal is not no runtime. The goal is runtime without framework fog.

intro.title: A different model for composition.
intro.description: Zenith separates concerns with more discipline: structure from behavior, compiler from runtime, ownership from convenience, and output from abstraction.

differenceIntro.label: Why it feels different
differenceIntro.title: Zenith tries to make composition more auditable instead of more magical.
differenceIntro.text: The goal is not to add another clever abstraction layer. It is to keep the relationship between authored templates, compiler decisions, and browser work easier to inspect.

comparisons.label: Structure vs Behavior
comparisons.leftText: Components describe how output is assembled and how composition is preserved.
comparisons.rightText: Runtime behavior is attached through signals, refs, events, and explicit hydration contracts.

comparisons.label: Compiler vs Runtime
comparisons.leftText: Zenith prefers compile-time guarantees where they improve determinism and reduce browser work.
comparisons.rightText: The browser gets the smallest necessary execution surface, not a general framework engine.

comparisons.label: Ownership vs Hidden Boundaries
comparisons.leftText: Slot content and local state do not quietly rebind to the nearest component boundary.
comparisons.rightText: Zenith avoids hidden ownership transfer that makes reactivity and composition harder to audit.

comparisons.label: Output vs Indirection
comparisons.leftText: SSR HTML, markers, and payloads are meant to remain understandable from the authored template.
comparisons.rightText: Zenith does not treat browser output as a disposable byproduct of framework internals.

differenceCards.title: More work resolves before boot
differenceCards.text: Zenith tries to remove work from runtime when that work can be determined safely during compilation.

differenceCards.title: Components stay structural
differenceCards.text: Component boundaries are there to shape output and preserve scope, not to generate hidden runtime ownership islands.

differenceCards.title: Reactive contracts stay explicit
differenceCards.text: Refs, signals, state, and event handlers remain visible parts of the authoring model instead of being inferred later.

differenceCards.title: Browser output stays inspectable
differenceCards.text: Zenith treats emitted HTML and marker tables as part of the framework contract, not as implementation debris.

intro.title: One public surface. A deliberately aligned stack.
intro.description: Projects import @zenithbuild/core. The inner packages stay implementation-side so the framework can evolve under one contract instead of many loosely related ones.
intro.noteText: The architecture is meant to feel unified from compiler to editor tooling. That is why the public surface stays smaller than the internal package graph.

summary.label: Shipped surfaces
summary.title: The architecture is broad, but the public story is intentionally simple.
summary.text: Zenith ships a compiler, runtime, router, bundler, CLI, language package, and language server. The framework treats them as one system because authors experience them as one system.

parts.name: @zenithbuild/core
parts.description: The single public import surface projects rely on.

parts.name: Compiler
parts.description: Resolves templates, markers, contracts, and deterministic output before the browser boots.

parts.name: Runtime
parts.description: Hydrates explicit payloads and applies narrow browser-side work through shipped markers and primitives.

parts.name: Router
parts.description: Owns route structure, guard/load boundaries, and page-level data contracts.

parts.name: Bundler
parts.description: Packages emitted HTML, JS, and CSS into the build output Zenith actually ships.

parts.name: CLI
parts.description: Coordinates scaffold, dev, build, and release-train workflows around compiler truth.

parts.name: Language
parts.description: Packages the VS Code-compatible extension path and bundled language server delivery.

parts.name: Language Server
parts.description: Delivers compiler-backed diagnostics, quick fixes, hover, and completion parity to LSP consumers.

tooling.label: Editor Experience
tooling.title: Tooling follows the same contract discipline.
tooling.description: Zenith’s editor story is not a separate approximation layer. The language server now surfaces compiler-backed diagnostics and the first stable quick fixes through the same rules the compiler already enforces.

features.title: Compiler-backed diagnostics
features.text: Script-boundary failures, invalid event handlers, unknown events, and DOM lint rules all flow from compiler truth.

features.title: ZEN-DOM quick fixes
features.text: The first stable code actions now cover DOM query, listener, and wrapper guidance without inventing editor-only rules.

features.title: Canonical hover and completion
features.text: Documented on:* events and core Zenith APIs surface through the same manifest-backed language server path.

features.title: Verified editor delivery
features.text: The packaged VS Code-compatible flow and direct Neovim LSP path were both validated against the shipped language server.

intro.title: Active, honest, and getting sharper.
intro.description: Zenith is far enough along to communicate a real architecture, real tooling, and real release discipline. It is also still evolving in public.

snapshot.label: Current line
snapshot.title: Stable should stay dependable while the next release train earns its place.
snapshot.text: Zenith is still being sharpened in public, but the release story is meant to stay clear: dependable stable installs, a train lane for validation, and beta only when it serves a deliberate experiment.

tracks.label: Latest
tracks.title: The stable line is for dependable installs.
tracks.text: Stable releases are the line users should reach for when they want the current dependable public version.

tracks.label: Train
tracks.title: Train is the validation lane.
tracks.text: Train is where upcoming stable work is published, verified, and promoted only after it proves itself coherently across the package set.

tracks.label: Beta
tracks.title: Beta stays intentional and opt-in.
tracks.text: Prerelease experimentation remains a deliberate maintainer action instead of an automatic side effect of every merge.

roadmap.title: Stronger compiler guarantees
roadmap.text: Keep tightening emission, marker, slot-scope, and ownership guarantees so the output contract stays trustworthy.

roadmap.title: Better editor tooling
roadmap.text: Extend diagnostics, quick fixes, and canonical language support without duplicating rules away from compiler truth.

roadmap.title: Cleaner app and content architecture
roadmap.text: Keep moving site and framework surfaces toward explicit content boundaries, deterministic build paths, and clearer ownership.

roadmap.title: Ecosystem maturity
roadmap.text: Grow the surrounding tooling and docs without diluting the compiler-first model that makes Zenith distinct.

intro.title: Zenith is being shaped in the open by people pulling at the contracts.
intro.description: The framework gets better when more than one perspective tests the compiler rules, runtime behavior, docs, and editor experience. This section is ready to grow into a fuller public roster as named credits land.
intro.noteText: The goal is not a decorative team strip. It is to show the real work that sharpens Zenith: building, testing, refining, and challenging the details.

roster.label: Current roster
roster.title: A live GitHub-backed roster instead of a hardcoded credits block.
roster.text: Contributor avatars, profiles, and aggregate contribution totals are loaded from the Zenith GitHub organization so this section can stay current as the public work expands.

emptyState.title: Contributor data is temporarily unavailable.
emptyState.text: This roster is loaded from the Zenith GitHub organization at request time and will repopulate when GitHub data is reachable again.

intro.title: Help fund the next layer of Zenith.
intro.description: Sponsorship helps keep the framework moving with the same discipline it asks from authored code: careful compiler work, strong tooling, clearer docs, and ecosystem maintenance that does not get treated as an afterthought.

pitch.label: Why sponsorship matters
pitch.text: Zenith is being built as one aligned system. Funding helps keep the core framework, editor work, documentation, release verification, and surrounding ecosystem effort moving forward together instead of in bursts.

reasons.title: Framework development
reasons.text: Compiler, runtime, router, and release-train work all need sustained maintenance.

reasons.title: Tooling
reasons.text: Language server work, diagnostics, and quick fixes need time to stay aligned with compiler truth.

reasons.title: Docs
reasons.text: Clearer documentation, examples, and migration guidance turn good architecture into usable practice.

reasons.title: Ecosystem work
reasons.text: Templates, starter flows, validation, and surrounding support work are what make a framework feel complete.

sponsor.supportNote: Direct sponsorship links are being finalized. The primary funding CTA is wired so a real sponsor URL can drop into content cleanly when it is ready.

finalCta.title: Start with the contracts, then follow the releases.
finalCta.text: If Zenith is useful to you, the best path is still the same: read the docs, inspect the changelog, and watch the framework sharpen in public.
